spec_match.rationale: Correctly demonstrates the standard 'none', 'all', 'leading', and 'trailing' values side by side.

interactive.rationale: The page is a static comparison grid. Aside from a basic JavaScript support check on page load, there are no controls, sliders, or inputs for user interaction.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Matches the parent index's description of comparing none, all, leading, and trailing side by side to see how underlines disappear or join.

edge_cases.rationale: It covers the wrapped-inline edge case nicely but does so using a static max-width layout, meaning users cannot test how wrap points change dynamic rendering.

references.rationale: Includes direct, highly helpful references to the ChromeStatus entry and the motivating CSSWG GitHub issue.

design_system.rationale: Aligned perfectly with design styles, standard borders, serif font selections, and stone backgrounds.

openQuestions.title: Add interactive paragraph width controls to test dynamic wrapping
openQuestions.detail: The page currently acts as a static reference layout. To make it genuinely interactive as required by the repository guidelines, we should implement a width slider (e.g. <input type='range'>) that lets users dynamically resize the width of the text sample blocks. This allows them to observe exactly how the underline breaks, overlaps, or joins at the wrap boundary as the text flows between lines in real-time.

summary: A well-motivated comparison page illustrating why the CSSWG introduced leading/trailing space-skipping controls. However, it is currently static, violating the repository's interactivity rule. Adding a responsive width controller so developers can watch the underline breaks recalculate in real-time will make it compliant and far more engaging.
